note_data.title: Narrative writing
keyPoints: Paper 1 Section 2 Composition offers candidates a choice of five essay titles: 1 descriptive, 2 argumentative, and 2 narrative essay titles
Candidates write a response of 350-500 words for composition tasks worth 30 marks
Assessment objective W1: Articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined
Assessment objective W2: Sequence facts, ideas and opinions
Assessment objective W3: Use a range of appropriate vocabulary
Assessment objective W4: Use register appropriate to audience and context
Assessment objective W5: Make accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar

In the Composition section, candidates have an opportunity to display their English language skills in order to express their opinion, experience or imagination in a range of discourse types: descriptive, argumentative or narrative
Language marking requires sentence structures varied for particular effects, verb forms largely correct and appropriate tenses consistently used

definitions.definition: Section 2 writing task where candidates complete one writing task from a choice of five essay titles, writing 350-500 words
Articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined
Use register appropriate to audience and context
15 marks allocated for good understanding of purpose, clear awareness of situation and audience, format entirely appropriate
15 marks allocated for accuracy, sentence structure variety, correct verb forms, wide vocabulary, accurate punctuation and spelling

howToAnswer.instruction: Create a complete narrative with clear structure, developed characters and engaging plot
Maintain the style, tone and characters established in the given opening
Create a detailed outline showing plot structure, character details and key events

modelAnswer.question: Write a story that begins: 'The electricity had been out for three hours when Fatima heard the footsteps on the roof.'
modelAnswer.answer: The electricity had been out for three hours when Fatima heard the footsteps on the roof. Her heart hammered against her ribs as she clutched her younger brother's hand in the suffocating darkness of their Lahore home. 'Probably just a cat,' she whispered, but her trembling voice betrayed her fear. The monsoon rain had stopped, leaving an eerie silence that made every creak and groan of the old house sound menacing. Another thud, deliberate and heavy. Definitely not a cat. Fatima's mind raced - their parents were at the hospital with grandmother, leaving her responsible for eight-year-old Hassan. She grabbed the torch from the kitchen drawer, its weak beam barely penetrating the gloom. As she crept toward the staircase leading to the roof terrace, Hassan whimpered behind her. The footsteps continued, slow and purposeful. Taking a deep breath, Fatima pushed open the door and stepped onto the wet terrace. 'Hello?' she called shakily. A figure emerged from behind the water tank - their elderly neighbor, Uncle Mahmood, clutching a flashlight and looking equally startled. 'Beta, I was checking my antenna after the storm,' he explained sheepishly. 'The power lines are down.' Relief flooded through Fatima as she began to laugh, the tension melting away like the puddles around their feet.
markBreakdown: Marks 1-5: Engaging opening that continues naturally
Marks 11-15: Clear plot structure with building tension
Marks 16-20: Effective use of descriptive language
Marks 21-25: Satisfying resolution with appropriate tone

commonMistakes: Commonly confused words, for example, their/there, seat/sit
Not writing in full sentences, even in informal writing
Not developing bullet points as evenly as possible in Section 1 tasks
examinerTips: It is essential to read the question carefully and be clear about its particular focus
Greater focus on correct use of possessive pronouns is strongly recommended
Concentration on correct tenses and agreement would improve candidates' writing in both sections
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Story mountain diagram showing exposition at base, rising action climbing up, climax at peak, falling action descending, and resolution at bottom right - essential for planning narrative structure
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q1 (40 marks) — they asked students to write a narrative beginning with 'The door creaked open and I saw something that changed my life forever'
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q2 (40 marks) — students had to continue a story starting with 'It was supposed to be an ordinary school day until...'
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (40 marks) — candidates wrote a narrative titled 'The Journey Home' focusing on character development
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q1 (40 marks) — students wrote a story where the main character discovers a hidden secret about their family
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P2 Q2 (40 marks) — candidates had to write a narrative from the perspective of an object witnessing important events

workedExample.question: Write a narrative beginning with: 'I never believed in second chances until that rainy Tuesday.' (40 marks)
steps: Step 1: Plan the narrative structure - establish protagonist who has given up on something important, create the conflict/challenge on Tuesday, plan the resolution showing growth
Step 2: Develop the opening - set scene immediately, establish character's cynical mindset about second chances through internal monologue and past experiences
Step 3: Build tension through the middle - introduce the unexpected opportunity/person/situation that challenges the character's beliefs, use dialogue and sensory details
Step 4: Create the climax - show the moment of decision where character must choose to take the second chance, use shorter sentences for pace
Step 5: Conclude with resolution - show character transformation, circle back to the opening theme, end with reflection on the lesson learned
workedExample.answer:
I never believed in second chances until that rainy Tuesday. The droplets hammered against my office window like tiny fists, each one reminding me of the opportunities I'd let slip away. My resignation letter sat on the desk—crisp, final, inevitable.

Then Sarah walked in.

'The Morrison project isn't dead,' she announced, shaking rainwater from her umbrella. 'They want to meet tomorrow.'

I stared at her, remembering the months of work, the crushing rejection, the sleepless nights that followed. 'Sarah, it's over. I'm done.'

'Are you?' She placed a folder on my desk. 'Because they specifically asked for you. Said your ideas were brilliant, just ahead of their time.'

Outside, the rain continued its relentless rhythm. Inside, something stirred—a forgotten ember of hope I thought had died. Maybe, just maybe, second chances weren't myths after all.

I tore up the resignation letter.
workedExample.markScheme: Marks awarded for: engaging opening that hooks reader (5 marks), well-developed characters with clear motivation (8 marks), effective use of dialogue and description (8 marks), coherent plot structure with clear beginning/middle/end (8 marks), strong conclusion that links to opening theme (6 marks), technical accuracy in grammar/spelling/punctuation (5 marks)

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to maintain consistent narrative perspective throughout their story' — always check whether you're writing in first person or third person and stick to it consistently

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often rushed the ending, providing weak conclusions that didn't resolve the central conflict' — always plan your ending first and ensure it provides satisfying resolution to the main problem

Examiner Report 2023: 'Students frequently included too many characters and plot points, making stories confusing and unfocused' — limit yourself to 2-3 main characters maximum and one central conflict for clarity and depth
note_data.keyDiagram: Students should be able to sketch a narrative structure diagram showing: 1) Exposition (introducing character/setting in opening 2-3 paragraphs), 2) Rising Action (building tension through 3-4 paragraphs with increasing conflict), 3) Climax (the turning point in 1-2 paragraphs where main character faces crucial decision), 4) Falling Action (consequences of the climax in 1-2 paragraphs), 5) Resolution (satisfying conclusion that shows character growth/change). This pyramid structure ensures proper pacing and prevents rushed or incomplete stories.
note_data.quickRecap: Paper 1 Section 2 Composition requires candidates to choose one task from five options including narrative essays, writing 350-500 words for 30 marks. Assessment focuses on expressing thoughts and imagination while using appropriate vocabulary and register. Language accuracy including correct tenses, sentence variety, and proper punctuation is essential for high marks.
